been used successfully. Oral itraconazole is often the most 
practical treatment in general practice. Ideally, for all treat-
ment regimes, confirmation of negative fungal status 
should be obtained using repeat testing (microscopic 
examination and fungal culture) after the clinical signs have 
resolved. In colony situations, eradication of disease may 
be difficult, even with antifungal treatment, disinfection 
and isolation. Owners should be made aware of the 
zoonotic potential of dermatophytosis.
Dermatitis due to Malassezia ovale is reported in 
guinea pigs, including in association with T. caviae infesta-
tion. Candida albicans has been shown experimentally to 
cause skin lesions in guinea pigs by cutaneous application, 
and there are anecdotal reports of cutaneous infections. 
Cryptococcus neoformans can cause ulcerative dermatitis, 
especially of the nose. Diagnosis is made by the detection  A guinea pig with flank alopecia due to cystic ovarian

Hyperadrenocorticism
ovarian disease.
Hyperadrenocorticism (see Chapter 22) has been reported 
in guinea pigs as a cause of non-pruritic alopecia and thin 
skin (Figure 14.4). Other clinical signs include polyuria,  Neoplastic skin disease

(a)
14.5 A guinea pig with a large ulcerated mammary tumour.
Trichofolliculomas are the most common cutaneous 
neoplasm seen. They are benign and usually solitary, and 
generally occur on the dorsum (Figure 14.6). There is no 
age or sex predilection. A central pore is often seen, 
through which keratinous or haemorrhagic material is 
discharged, which may be confused visually with pus. 
Cytological examination can confirm the nature of the 
discharge if necessary, with the absence of inflammatory 
cells and bacteria indicating a trichofolliculoma. Tumours 
can be large (up to 4 cm or more in diameter), usually 
continue to grow in size, and can ulcerate and rupture, so 
surgical excision is recommended, preferably before they 
progress to this stage.
Lipomas are reported to be the second most common 
cutaneous neoplasm, and may be associated with fibrosis 
(b) and steatitis. They can occur anywhere on the body but the 
(a, b) A guinea pig with truncal symmetrical alopecia due  inguinal area is a common site; masses here should be dif-

Chapter 14 • Skin disease
Other nutritional deficiencies are rare in pet guinea pigs 
but have been produced experimentally. Protein deficiency 
causes a generalized alopecia. Fatty acid and pyridoxine 
deficiency result in alopecia, scaling and dermatitis.
Environmental and behavioural skin 
conditions
Stress
Stressed or ill guinea pigs frequently shed large amounts 
of hair (telogen defluxion). This is possibly due in part to 
an increased requirement for vitamin C.
Fur chewing and barbering
Fur chewing and barbering (of conspecifics or self) can 
be associated with a lack of fibre in the diet, stress or 
overcrowding, or may be performed over a painful area. 
The owners may observe the behaviour directly or simply 
14.6 A guinea pig with trichofolliculoma. see areas of non-pruritic hair loss on some or all animals.

Nutritional skin disease
Hypovitaminosis C is common in guinea pigs and underlies 
many disease processes (see Chapters 2 and 22). It should 
always be considered as a factor in dermatological cases, 
particularly in bacterial, fungal and ectoparasitic skin dis-
ease. Important early cutaneous signs are a roughened 
hair coat and scaling of the pinnae. Inadequate dietary 
levels of vitamin C or anorexia will rapidly lead to clinical 
signs. Diagnosis is usually based on clinical signs and 
dietary history as, although possible, measurement of 
serum vitamin C concentration is not usually performed 
on companion guinea pigs due to the volume of blood 
required. Treatment involves correcting the diet and giving 
additional supplementation of vitamin C (50–100 mg/kg/
day) until the clinical signs resolve, alongside specific  Plantar pododermatitis of the hind feet. Erythema and

Hyperkeratosis and cutaneous horns can develop on the 
footpads, especially in heavy or obese guinea pigs and 
Miscellaneous conditions
those housed in wire-bottomed cages. The horns can be 
clipped or filed away, and appropriate substrate and hus-
Scent gland impaction
bandry changes should be made.
Guinea pigs possess sebaceous scent glands on the rump 
and perineal area. In boars, the oily secretions make the
